worth_it_explanation: Demand Manager carries the header-bidding auction that runs before the ad server is called, so its two main levers, the wrapper timeout and the set of enabled bidders, sit directly on the line between fill rate, page speed, and the value collected per impression. A timeout set too short cuts bids out of the auction, and one set too long delays the ad server call and slows the page. A bidder list that grew without pruning adds latency for adapters that rarely win. This check is a review of a configuration the publisher already runs in Demand Manager, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete setting the publisher can test against revenue and page-speed data.
intro: Magnite Demand Manager is a managed Prebid header-bidding wrapper that runs a client-side and server-side auction for a publisher's inventory before the ad server is called. This subcategory covers the configuration that shapes that auction, the wrapper timeout and the set of enabled bidders, rather than the floor pricing or the ad-server line items that sit downstream. These checks confirm that the timeout balances fill against latency on evidence rather than a default, and that every enabled bidder earns its place with server-side and client-side placement chosen on purpose.

audit_checks.title: Wrapper timeout is left at a default rather than tuned to the inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The wrapper timeout sets how long the header-bidding auction runs before the ad server is called, so it controls the trade between collecting more bids and delaying the page. A timeout set too short drops slower bids from the auction and lowers fill, and one set too long delays the ad server call and slows page load, which can cost both revenue and user experience. A default value left unexamined misses the balance point for the publisher's own audience and inventory.

educate.base:
Magnite Demand Manager runs on Prebid, so its auction timeout works the same way Prebid's does. The auction timeout sets the amount of time the header-bidding auction is allowed to run before the ad server is called. A bidder that responds inside the window joins the auction, and a bidder that responds after it is cut out. The timeout is the single setting that decides how much of the demand actually competes for each impression.

Setting the timeout is a balance. Too short, and slower bidders never make it into the auction, so fill rate and competition drop and impressions clear below what they could have. Too long, and the header-bidding auction holds up the ad server call, which delays the ad and slows the page, and a slow page can cost both revenue and the audience's patience. Prebid's own guidance frames the value as a delicate balance for exactly this reason, and notes that the right number depends on the publisher's audience, network conditions, and revenue mix rather than a single figure that fits everyone.

A managed wrapper such as Demand Manager ships with a default timeout, and a default is a starting point rather than a tuned value. The audit question is whether the publisher arrived at the current timeout by reading their own fill-rate and page-speed data, or whether the default has simply stayed in place. There is also a second timeout to confirm. Where bidders run server-side through Prebid Server, the server timeout should sit below the wrapper auction timeout so server-side bids return in time to join the auction.

A few signs point to a timeout left at a default rather than tuned:
  • The wrapper auction timeout matches the product default and no record shows it was tested against the publisher's own data
  • Bid-response timing data shows a large share of bidders responding just after the timeout cutoff, which suggests the window is too short for the inventory
  • Page-speed measurement shows the ad server call waiting on the auction longer than the audience tolerates, which suggests the window is too long
You may want to confirm that the timeout was set on evidence rather than inherited from the default. One approach is to read the share of bids arriving before and after the current cutoff alongside page-load timing, then test a shorter or longer timeout and compare fill rate, revenue, and load time across both.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, readers often arrive from social or search on mobile connections that respond more slowly, so a timeout tuned for fast desktop traffic can cut bids out for the audience that actually shows up. You may want to read bid-response timing split by device and connection, then set a timeout that lets the real audience's bids land rather than one tuned for the fastest case.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, page speed feeds directly into conversion, so a long auction timeout that delays the page can cost sales as well as ad revenue. You may want to weigh the fill gained from a longer timeout against the page-speed cost on the commercial pages specifically, where a slower load carries a higher price than it does on editorial pages.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed configuration documentation for Demand Manager is in the partner portal (login required). The public Prebid documentation above describes the same timeout mechanism Demand Manager runs on.

investigate.intro: Review the wrapper auction timeout against the publisher's bid-response timing and page-speed data, and confirm any server-side timeout sits below the auction timeout.

demand_manager_ui.label: Magnite Demand Manager console
steps: Open the Demand Manager configuration for the publisher's account and record the wrapper auction timeout value in place.
Note whether the timeout matches the product default or a value the publisher set, and ask whether any test or before-and-after read informed it.
If bidders run server-side through Prebid Server, record the server timeout and confirm it sits below the wrapper auction timeout so server-side bids return in time.

demand_manager_reporting.label: Magnite Demand Manager reporting
steps: Build a report on bid-response timing and note the share of bids that arrive before the current timeout cutoff versus those that arrive after it.
Read fill rate and average eCPM alongside the timing data to see whether bids are being cut out at the current window.
Measure page-load timing for the ad slots and note whether the ad server call is waiting on the auction longer than the audience tolerates.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the wrapper auction timeout, the share of bids arriving before and after the cutoff, page-load timing, and any server-side timeout. A timeout set on the publisher's own fill-rate and page-speed data, with the server timeout sitting below the auction timeout, passes. A timeout left at the product default with no test on record, or one where a large share of bids land just after the cutoff or the page waits longer than the audience tolerates, is a fail recorded with the timing data that shows the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the wrapper timeout, the bid-response timing, and the page-load timing above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs its Demand Manager wrapper on a timeout that matches the product default rather than one tuned to its own data. The auction timeout sets how long the header-bidding auction runs before the ad server is called, so a window too short drops slower bids and lowers fill, and one too long delays the page and can cost both revenue and the audience's patience. {{voice_recommend}} reading the share of bids that arrive before and after the current cutoff alongside page-load timing, then testing a shorter or longer timeout and comparing fill rate, revenue, and load time across both. Where bidders run server-side, confirm the server timeout sits below the wrapper auction timeout so server-side bids return in time to compete.

audit_checks.title: Enabled bidders are not justified and server-side placement is not deliberate
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Each enabled bidder adds a request to the auction, and a bidder that rarely wins still adds latency and data weight for every impression it sits in. A list that grew without pruning slows the auction without lifting revenue, and running every bidder client-side rather than placing low-priority bidders server-side loads that latency onto the user's device. Both leave the auction slower than the revenue it returns justifies.

educate.base:
Every bidder enabled in Demand Manager adds a request to the header-bidding auction. More bidders mean more competition, and more competition generally lifts yield, so adding bidders is not wrong on its own. The cost is that each bidder also adds time and data weight, and the lift from adding bidders falls off. Prebid's own analysis shows revenue rising as bidders are added, then flattening, and even dropping where the page waits too long for the extra demand to respond. Past a point, another bidder adds latency without adding revenue.

That makes a bidder list something to justify rather than to grow without limit. A bidder that has been in the configuration for months without winning meaningful revenue is still adding a request to every auction it sits in, slowing the page and, where send-all-bids is on, adding targeting data sent to the ad server for a bidder that rarely clears. The audit question is whether each enabled bidder earns its place on win rate and revenue, or whether the list has accumulated adapters that no longer pull their weight.

Placement is the second lever. Prebid supports running bidders client-side, where the request runs in the browser, or server-side through Prebid Server, where the requests run server-to-server and the browser carries less of the work. The common pattern is a hybrid, where the highest-value bidders keep a client-side connection and lower-priority or newly-tested bidders run server-side so they compete without burdening the device. The same bidder cannot run on both paths at once, so each bidder's placement is a deliberate choice. A setup that runs every bidder client-side has not made that choice and loads all of the latency onto the browser.

A few signs point to an unjustified bidder list or undeliberate placement:
  • Bidders sit in the configuration with win rates and revenue near zero, with no record of why they remain enabled
  • Every bidder runs client-side with no server-side path, so all of the auction's latency lands on the user's device
  • The publisher cannot say which bidders are kept for revenue, which for testing, and why each runs on the path it does
You may want to confirm that each enabled bidder is justified and placed on purpose. One approach is to read win rate and revenue per bidder, retire or move the adapters that rarely win, and move low-priority bidders to the server-side path so the browser carries only the highest-value demand.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a long bidder list often reflects partnerships added over time rather than a list pruned to performance, and every adapter that rarely wins still slows the page for readers on mobile connections. You may want to read win rate per bidder, retire the adapters that no longer earn their place, and move lower-priority bidders to the server-side path so the reader's browser carries less of the auction.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the latency each redundant bidder adds feeds into page speed and so into conversion, so a bloated client-side list carries a cost beyond ad revenue. You may want to keep only the highest-value bidders client-side on the commercial pages and push the rest to the server-side path, so the auction stays competitive without slowing the pages closest to a purchase.

learn_more.source_title: How Many Bidders Should I Work With?

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed Demand Manager bidder and placement documentation is in the partner portal (login required). The public Prebid documentation above describes the bidder-count trade-off and the server-side path Demand Manager runs on.

investigate.intro: Review the enabled bidders against their win rate and revenue, and confirm that client-side versus server-side placement is a deliberate choice rather than every bidder running in the browser.

demand_manager_ui.label: Magnite Demand Manager console
steps: Open the Demand Manager configuration and list every enabled bidder along with whether it runs client-side or server-side.
Note any bidder running on both paths, which is not supported, and record which bidders have no server-side option set.
Ask the publisher which bidders are kept for revenue, which are kept for testing, and why each runs on the path it does.

demand_manager_reporting.label: Magnite Demand Manager reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by bidder with bid requests, win rate, and revenue.
Identify bidders with win rates and revenue near zero that still send a request on every auction they sit in.

Compare the highest-revenue bidders' placement against the lowest, and note whether high-value bidders run client-side and low-priority bidders run server-side or whether placement looks undeliberate.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each enabled bidder's win rate, revenue, and placement. A list where each bidder earns its place on revenue, the highest-value bidders run client-side, and lower-priority bidders run server-side passes. A list carrying bidders with win rates near zero and no stated reason, or one running every bidder client-side with no server-side path, is a fail recorded with the bidders that no longer pull their weight and the placement that loads the device.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each bidder's win rate, revenue, and client-side or server-side placement above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs a Demand Manager bidder list that has not been justified on performance, with low-winning bidders still in the auction and placement not chosen on purpose. Each enabled bidder adds a request, and a bidder that rarely wins still adds latency and data weight, while running every bidder client-side loads that latency onto the user's device. {{voice_recommend}} reading win rate and revenue per bidder, retiring or moving the adapters that rarely win, and placing the highest-value bidders client-side while moving lower-priority bidders to the server-side path through Prebid Server so the browser carries only the demand worth its weight. After the change, recheck fill rate, revenue, and page-load timing to confirm the leaner list holds competition while lightening the page.
